summary.likely_root_cause: cron wrapper budget too small for intermittent network/auth work

jobs.name: gravix-aggregate-knowledge

last_runs.note: near the timeout ceiling even when successful

findings: Timeout failures clamp almost exactly at 60s, which points to wrapper/runtime budget, not random app exceptions.
One successful run landed at 53.8s, proving the job can get dangerously close to the ceiling under normal conditions.
The underlying Gravix cron endpoints already log to cron_run_log in the API, so backend-side timing can be inspected separately tomorrow.
next_actions: Bump cron timeout budget above 60s for this job before changing business logic.
Split summary work from endpoint execution if the cron framework is spending part of the 60s budget on narration/delivery.
Query Gravix cron_run_log for the matching timestamps to confirm whether the backend finished after the wrapper timed out or never reached completion.

jobs.name: gravix-send-followups
findings: Success cases sit around 10-34s, including days when real followup emails were sent.
Failures are again pinned at ~60s, same signature as the other broken jobs.
Because this job can send actual emails, extending budget is lower risk than changing the followup query/sending logic blind.
next_actions: If failures persist, instrument send_pending_followups() with per-phase timing: candidate fetch, email render, send loop, final DB update.
Check whether any provider call is blocking when there are zero sends due, since healthy runs with sent:0 still sometimes take 20-30s.

jobs.name: moneysamurai-sync-trigger
findings: This job is the noisiest: many successes between 25-56s and many failures pinned at ~60s.
The business action usually succeeds once the script gets through auth and trigger POST; failures correlate with a long preflight chain rather than trigger rejection.
Historical summaries show schema mismatches in cleanup logic: code tried public.sync_status and sync_jobs.error_message, but working paths were amazon_accounts.sync_status and sync_jobs.error.
That mismatch likely adds avoidable retries/noise and burns precious time inside the 60s wrapper.
next_actions: Trim the preflight/reset path so it only touches known-good tables/columns.
Keep auth-token generation and trigger POST, but move optional cleanup to a best-effort fast path or separate maintenance job.
Raise timeout budget above 60s anyway, because even successful runs routinely exceed 45s.
